prompt: Build a CareOps Service Billing & Quality Management Portal that runs entirely inside a provider’s local network to manage in-home care service catalogs, package billing, staff performance, and operational reporting. Users sign in with a locally verified username and password and land in a Dioxus-based web UI that adapts by role: System Administrator configures organizations, departments, and projects; Operations Manager maintains the service catalog (nursing, rehab, meals, companionship) and builds packages with per-visit, hourly, and tiered rules; Billing Specialist reviews generated charges, posts adjustments, and processes refunds; Coach/Clinician records delivered units and eligibility notes; QA Reviewer performs second reviews on graded evaluations; Auditor has read-only access with masked exports. The UI supports task-focused screens for creating a service package, assigning it to a client plan, capturing delivery entries, and generating invoices and reconciliation summaries; every action gives immediate feedback with inline validation (for example, labor hours must be in 0.25-hour increments, and mileage reimbursement caps at 200 miles per visit). Reporting screens provide order volume, issued service tickets, utilization/load factor, revenue, and refund rate by date range and service route (client-to-clinic or provider region), plus KPI analytics such as attendance rate, repurchase rate, and staff utilization, with CSV export that respects permission isolation. A built-in scoring workflow supports objective auto-scoring and subjective manual grading with partial credit, comments, and a required second review for any score change over 10 points, with progress indicators and configurable rounding (nearest 0.5) and weights by question type. The backend uses Rocket to expose offline REST-style APIs consumed by the Dioxus frontend, enforcing a fine-grained RBAC model that links roles to permissions at menu/button/API levels and applies data-scope rules by organization, department, and project; permission changes take effect within 30 seconds via an in-process cache with version checks to prevent stale authorization. MySQL persists users, roles, audit trails, service catalog items, package rules, delivery records, invoices, refunds, grading artifacts, and report definitions; sensitive fields such as client identifiers and notes are encrypted at rest, and exports mask identifiers by default unless an Auditor-level permission is granted. Payment is modeled as an internal “recorded payment” and “recorded refund” closed loop (no external gateways): postings are idempotent using a locally generated reference key, duplicate submissions within 5 minutes are rejected, and partial refunds are allowed up to the net paid amount with mandatory reason codes; all reversals link to immutable fund-transaction records. Observability is delivered locally with structured logs, request tracing IDs, metrics snapshots, health checks, configuration-change auditing, and alert rules (for example, error rate over 2% for 10 minutes triggers a local dashboard alarm); one-click degradation toggles can disable exports and heavy analytics, and scheduled chaos drills simulate DB latency and API timeouts in a controlled window (e.g., Sundays 2:00–2:15 AM) to validate resilience without internet dependencies.
